lines.text: I'll be brief on this segment because much of what I'd say is changing as we speak, but I want to give you a sense of where the field is headed.
Pathology — the practice of looking at tissue under a microscope to diagnose disease — has been done essentially the same way for over a century.
A trained human eye looks at stained slides, recognizes patterns, makes a diagnosis.
It's also slow, inconsistent between observers, and limited to what the human can see.
First, whole-slide imaging — high-resolution digitization of pathology slides — has made it possible to store and transmit slides as digital images.
Second, deep learning, especially the convolutional and transformer models that drive modern computer vision, has gotten remarkably good at finding patterns in those images.

Combine the two, and you can train AI systems on hundreds of thousands of digitized pathology slides with known outcomes, and the systems learn to recognize patterns that even experienced pathologists miss.

There are now AI tools that, given a slide, can predict tumor grade more consistently than human pathologists, identify subtle patterns of cancer that correlate with outcomes, and even — and this is wild — predict which mutations a tumor is likely to carry, just by looking at the morphology.
The visual pattern of the tissue encodes more information than we used to be able to extract.
Similar revolutions are underway in radiology, where AI tools read CT and MRI scans, often catching nodules and lesions that human radiologists missed.

And in drug discovery, where AI systems like AlphaFold can predict the three-dimensional structure of a protein from its amino acid sequence — a problem we had been chipping away at for fifty years and that AlphaFold essentially solved in 2020.
Knowing protein structures faster means designing targeted drugs faster.
A useful framing for all of this: AI in oncology isn't replacing the pathologist or the oncologist; it's giving them new senses.
A pathologist with AI assistance can review more slides faster, with more consistent grading, and with predictions about molecular features that previously required separate genetic testing.
An oncologist with AI assistance can integrate genomics, imaging, pathology, and clinical features in ways no individual human could juggle in their head.
Whether these tools will fundamentally redefine the practice of oncology or just incrementally improve it is an open question, but the trajectory is clear.
